feature_name: Dual operation modes: Root Complex and End Point
description: The PCIe subsystem can operate as a Root Complex (RC) or End Point (EP). Mode selection is via bootstrap pins PCIESSMODE[1:0] and can be overridden in software. The core supports legacy EP and native EP behavior. Initialization sequences are documented separately for RC and EP roles. Software starts link training by setting CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]. The design targets single RC/EP link usage.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; Section 1.4.1; Section 2.11 Operations; CMD_STATUS register (3.1.3)
dependencies: DEVSTAT.PCIESSMODE, CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN], PCIE SerDes clock present
Remarks: Select role early; ensure SerDes PLL lock before enabling LTSSM.

feature_name: Link width and lane management (x1/x2, dynamic width, auto lane reversal)
description: The controller exposes a single bidirectional link with up to two lanes (x2). It supports dynamic width conversion and automatic lane reversal to improve robustness. Link width capability is reported in LINK_CAP and configured with PL_LINK_CTRL[LNK_MODE]. Negotiated link width is readable from LINK_STAT_CTRL. Hardware handles lane polarity inversion on receive. These mechanisms enable flexible board routing.
dependencies: SerDes lanes routed; PL_LINK_CTRL[LNK_MODE], LINK_CAP/MAX_LINK_WIDTH
Remarks: Keep board skew within spec; verify negotiated width via LINK_STAT_CTRL before high traffic.

feature_name: Gen1/Gen2 link speeds (2.5/5.0 GT/s) with directed speed change
description: The PHY and core support 2.5 GT/s (Gen1) and 5.0 GT/s (Gen2). PLL output stays at 2.5 GHz with internal bus width conversion for Gen2. Software can initiate a Gen2 speed change using PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD] and program LINK_CTRL2[TGT_SPEED]. Speed capability is advertised in LINK_CAP[MAX_LINK_SPEED]. This allows performance scaling based on the peer's capability.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; Section 2.3.3; PL_GEN2 (3.9.13); LINK_CTRL2 (3.7.13); LINK_CAP (3.7.5)
dependencies: Stable refclk; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL MPY set; PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD; LINK_CTRL2.TGT_SPEED
Remarks: After link enters L0, request directed speed change; monitor DEBUG/LINK_STAT for completion.

feature_name: VBUSM master/slave interfaces (128-bit) to SoC fabric
description: The PCIe module exposes one 128-bit VBUSM master port and one 128-bit VBUSM slave port. The master port sources inbound transactions into the SoC, while the slave port accepts outbound accesses and MMR accesses. This split enables high-throughput data ingress and egress. The SoC interconnect provides the clocks for these ports. No special configuration is needed for the VBUSM clock.
dependencies: SoC interconnect present; proper clocking of VBUSM domain
Remarks: Dimension interconnect and EDMA bandwidth for target PCIe rate (see Section 2.9).

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions, 1–8 MB granularity)
description: Outbound translation remaps internal bus addresses to PCIe memory space. Thirty-two equal-size regions are selectable via OB_SIZE (1, 2, 4, or 8 MB). Each region uses OB_OFFSET_INDEXn/OB_OFFSETn_HI to form 32/64-bit PCIe bases. Software enables translation with CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]. This supports flexible host-visible memory windows.
dependencies: Program OB_SIZE and all used OB_OFFSET_*; set CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]
Remarks: Avoid TLPs that cross region boundaries; size large buffers to region size to prevent undefined behavior.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions mapped by BARs)
description: Inbound translation maps accepted PCIe addresses into SoC address space. Four regions (0–3) are associated to BARs using IB_BARn and programmed with IB_STARTn_LO/HI and IB_OFFSETn. BAR0 is always mapped to application registers and cannot be remapped. Software enables inbound translation with CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]. This allows precise placement of EP-accessible buffers.
evidence: Section 2.7.2; BAR0 exception (2.7.2.2); IB_* registers (3.1.82–3.1.97); CMD_STATUS (3.1.3)
dependencies: BAR setup; IB_BARn/IB_START*/IB_OFFSET*; CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]
Remarks: Associate regions only with data BARs; for 64-bit BARs use paired BARs as specified.

feature_name: BARs and BAR Mask registers (host window sizing)
description: BAR0–BAR5 implement memory and I/O apertures per Type 0/1 headers. BAR Mask registers overlay the BARs when CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2] is set, letting on-chip software control size/type before enumeration. BAR0 is constrained to app registers and only bits [31:20] are configurable. BAR masks are write-only and must be tested via BAR writeback. This mechanism defines host-visible resource sizes deterministically.
dependencies: Enable DBI_CS2; program BARx_MASK then BARx; clear DBI_CS2 before RC enumeration
Remarks: Never change BAR masks from the link side; validate mask by writing test patterns as recommended.

feature_name: Address Space 0 windows (app regs, local/remote cfg, remote I/O)
description: A 16 KB Address Space 0 is divided into four 4 KB regions. It contains PCIESS application registers, local configuration space, a remote configuration access window, and an RC-only remote I/O window. Accesses must be single 32-bit and non-cacheable. Remote configuration and I/O accesses require an operational link. The IOBASE register supplies the base for outbound I/O TLPs.
dependencies: Operational link for remote windows; configure IOBASE for I/O TLP base
Remarks: Guard against accesses when link is down; add link-state checks in driver before remote ops.

feature_name: Endian conversion for inbound and outbound transactions
description: The PCIESS supports big- and little-endian systems despite the PCIe core being little-endian at pins. In big-endian systems, ENDIAN[1:0] programs swap granularity for inbound and outbound paths. Tables in the spec define swizzle modes for 1-, 2-, 4-, and 8-byte groupings. Byte enables are swapped consistently with data. This ensures coherency across heterogeneous endianness.
dependencies: System boot endian setting; program ENDIAN prior to traffic
Remarks: Validate with known patterns; align buffers to swap granularity to reduce overhead.

feature_name: Transaction size, alignment, and byte-strobe requirements
description: Outbound internal bus transactions are limited to 128 bytes and must be aligned; misaligned starts reduce max size to 120 bytes. Writes require a single contiguous run of byte enables without gaps. Only incremental bursts are supported; wrap/fixed bursts are not. Read interleaving is avoided if read sizes do not exceed the configured maximum. These rules prevent bus lockups and ensure predictable throughput.
evidence: Section 2.10.1 (Bus Mastering, Address Alignment, Burst Type, Read Interleaving, Byte Strobe)
dependencies: Configure MAX_REQ_SZ/MAX_PAYLD appropriately; DMA segmentation to 128B aligned chunks
Remarks: Enforce alignment and continuous byte enables in the driver and EDMA setup.

feature_name: DMA usage model via SoC EDMA and PCIESS master/slave
description: The PCIESS has no embedded DMA but integrates with SoC EDMA and its own master interface. In RC mode, EDMA can source/sink outbound/inbound memory transactions to endpoints. In EP mode, PCIESS master issues accesses to SoC memory for inbound TLPs, while software or EDMA drives outbound transfers. Bandwidth targets are ~85% of 2.0/4.0 Gbps per lane at Gen1/Gen2 after 8b/10b. Example PARAM setups are provided for EDMA read/write.
dependencies: Enable BUS_MASTER in STATUS_COMMAND; provision EDMA channels and interconnect
Remarks: Size EDMA aCnt/bCnt to payload; ensure outbound region mapping; consider PCIe flow control backpressure.

feature_name: MSI interrupt generation and reception (32 vectors)
description: The module supports MSI generation (EP) and reception (RC, and EP-to-EP via BAR0) with up to 32 vectors. MSI_CAP enables MSI; MSI_LOW32/UP32 and MSI_DATA define the target and data. Incoming MSIs set banked MSIn_IRQ_STATUS bits, gated by MSIn_IRQ_ENABLE_SET/CLR, and require IRQ_EOI to finish. EP writes to RC's MSI_IRQ register at BAR0 to inject the vector. This scales beyond legacy INTx and reduces interrupt latency.
dependencies: Enable MSI_CAP.MSI_EN; program MSI address/data; gate MSIn enables; IRQ_EOI handling
Remarks: Use distinct vectors per event; clear MSIn status then write IRQ_EOI to avoid retrigger hazards.

feature_name: Legacy INTx generation (EP) and reception (RC)
description: Legacy INTA–INTD are supported: EP can assert/deassert via EP_IRQ_SET/CLR and RC receives per-INT legacy status. Legacy enable/status/clear registers exist for A–D groups. INTx is disabled when MSI is enabled. INT_PIN in config indicates the used INTx line. RC software must probe downstream devices to identify the source upon receipt.
dependencies: Configure INT_PIN; do not enable MSI and INTx simultaneously; EP uses EP_IRQ_SET/CLR
Remarks: Work around EOI vs deassert races by reading downstream INT status before issuing EOI.

feature_name: Interrupt map and controller integration (14 events)
description: Fourteen interrupt lines from PCIESS map MSI banks, legacy groups, error, power/reset, and link events. Table 2-10 enumerates events and their meanings for RC and EP. ERR_* and PMRST_* registers present status/enables for error and power/reset classes. An EOI write completes CPU interrupt service. This structure enables fine-grained interrupt policy.
dependencies: SoC interrupt controller routing; enable only required event groups
Remarks: Map critical events (link down, AER) to high-priority lines; ensure EOI matches the event index.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) and baseline error handling
description: Baseline errors are exposed in STATUS_COMMAND and DEV_STAT_CTRL, with enables for Correctable, Non-Fatal, Fatal, and UR reporting. AER provides fine-grained status, mask, and severity (PCIE_UNCERR*, PCIE_CERR*, PCIE_ACCR) plus header logging (HDR_LOG0–3). RC consolidates error messages in ROOT_ERR_* and captures source IDs. Software can tune severity masks and ECRC generation/check to match system policy.
dependencies: Enable DEV_STAT_CTRL *RP bits; configure PCIE_*_MASK/SVRTY; optionally PCIE_ACCR ECRC bits
Remarks: Disable ECRC if peers lack support (note in 2.16.2.3.1); clear sticky W1C bits after handling to avoid repeats.

feature_name: Device power states and PME (D0/D1/D2/D3hot/cold)
description: The PCIe power model supports D0, optional D1/D2, D3hot, and D3cold. PMCAP advertises supported states and PME generation sources, while PM_CTL_STAT controls PWR_STATE and PME enable/status. Transition guidelines require quiescing outstanding TLPs and may optionally avoid internal reset on D3hot→D0 via NO_SOFT_RST. RC tracks PME requesters using ROOT_STATUS.
dependencies: System power rails active; driver sequences PM_CTL_STAT and acknowledges PME
Remarks: Ensure software drains transactions before entering D1/D2/D3hot; reinitialize as needed on resume.

feature_name: ASPM L0s/L1 and link power management controls
description: Active State Power Management is supported for L0s and L1 states. LINK_STAT_CTRL[ACTIVE_LINK_PM] selects allowed states, with COMMON_CLK_CFG influencing exit latencies. ACK_FREQ provides programmable L0s/L1 entry latencies and NFTS parameters. Hardware also supports software-driven entry to L1 via device D-states. This reduces idle power while balancing exit timing.
dependencies: Common reference clock recommended; set ACK_FREQ latencies and NFTS/COMM_NFTS
Remarks: Tune entry/exit latencies per topology; verify no throughput penalties under expected idle patterns.

feature_name: Clocking and common clock/SSC support
description: The PHY consumes a differential reference clock and can operate with or without common clock architecture. With common clock or SSC, software must program LINK_STAT_CTRL[COMMON_CLK_CFG] and expect longer training when clocks are independent. SerDes PLL settings (PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL) tailor loop bandwidth and multiplier to refclk. PCIe operation depends on PLL lock signaled by PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK].
dependencies: Stable refclk per PCIe CEM; program LB/MPY; check PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK] before MMIO
Remarks: If SSC used, ensure both link partners share REFCLK; increase training sequences when clocks are independent.

feature_name: Loopback testing: PIPE and PHY loopback
description: PIPE loopback allows master/slave roles for symbol-level validation without payload returns. PHY loopback connects TX to RX internally and can be used in RC mode to test TLP paths without a partner. Software uses PL_LINK_CTRL[LPBK_EN] for PIPE and SERDES_CFG0/1 TX/RX loopback fields for PHY, plus PL_FORCE_LINK to force states. Constraints require enabling loopback before traffic and avoiding config-type TLPs in PHY loopback.
dependencies: Set loopback bits; force link to POLL_ACTIVE for PHY loopback; BAR0/1 address precautions
Remarks: Enable prior to any TLP to avoid sequence number mismatches; restrict to supported TLP types per mode.

feature_name: Reset and hot reset handling
description: The design supports power-on reset, warm/system reset, and software-initiated downstream hot reset. RSTCMD[INIT_RST] initiates a hot reset; FLUSH_N indicates pending traffic. After any reset, software must wait at least 100 ms before transactions, and up to 1.5 s before giving up on non-responding devices. Power domain cycling resets the module and requires full reinitialization and link training.
dependencies: Quiesce traffic; RSTCMD sequencing; SoC PSC for power domain
Remarks: On link down requests, PCIESS auto-disables LTSSM and errors out queues; issue local reset and re-init.

feature_name: LTSSM and PHY status debug registers
description: DEBUG0/DEBUG1 expose LTSSM state, PIPE data/control, training/reset indicators, and link-up flags. Appendix A maps LTSSM encoded values to human-readable states. These registers are read-only and aid bring-up and fault triage. They indicate electrical idle, skip events, and TS1/TS2 reception. Using them, software can build robust link-state machines and diagnostics.
dependencies: None beyond access to app reg space
Remarks: Poll during training and error recovery; log LTSSM transitions for failure analysis.

feature_name: Port/link control and Gen2 electrical tuning
description: PL_LINK_CTRL configures link enable, loopback, and reset inject, while LINK_CTRL2 controls Gen2 target speed, compliance entry, de-emphasis, and margins. PL_GEN2 adds directed speed change, lane enables, and TX swing control. Together they provide comprehensive knobs for link bring-up and signal integrity optimization. Changes should be applied with awareness of the LTSSM and link state. Incorrect settings can force recovery or disable the link.
dependencies: Apply only in appropriate states (e.g., L0); observe LINK_STAT/DLL_ACTIVE and DEBUG
Remarks: Keep SI settings consistent with board design; document default values for field returns.

feature_name: PCIe message transmit/receive support
description: The controller can send and receive PCIe Messages (with or without data). PL_OMSG and PL_LINK_CTRL[OMSG_REQ] allow software-triggered message emission with a programmed payload. Message reception is handled by the core per PCIe base spec. This supports PME and other architectural messages. Filtering controls are available for vendor messages.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; PL_OMSG (3.9.3); SYMTIMER_FLTMASK vendor MSG bits (3.9.9)
dependencies: PL_OMSG payload and OMSG_REQ; filter mask configuration as needed
Remarks: Use for standard messages; vendor MSGs can be dropped or forwarded per filter configuration.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and status
description: Chip-level PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL sets PLL multiply, loop bandwidth, and enables the PLL; PCIE_SERDES_STS reports lock and lane test status. Proper refclk selection and LB setting optimize jitter tolerance. ENPLL must be set, followed by a stabilization delay and LOCK polling. These registers are protected by a device-specific kicker mechanism before writes. Stable PLL lock is a prerequisite for any PCIe operation.
dependencies: Kicker unlock (device-specific); valid refclk; program MPY/LB/ENPLL; wait for LOCK
Remarks: Use device data manual for exact addresses; never initiate transactions before LOCK is high.

feature_name: Signal interface and pinout (no pin multiplexing)
description: The subsystem presents differential TX/RX pairs per lane and differential reference clock pins PCIECLKP/N. Signal table lists lane pins PCIERXP/N[0:1] and PCIETXP/N[0:1]. PCIESS functional pins are not multiplexed with other signals. Power, grounds, and terminations must follow the PHY requirements. Correct board layout and termination ensure signal integrity.
dependencies: Board-level differential routing and terminations per TI guidelines
Remarks: Observe length matching and impedance control; adhere to refclk quality specifications.

feature_name: BAR0 inbound exception to application registers
description: Inbound transactions that hit BAR0 are always routed to the PCIESS application registers in both RC and EP. This mapping cannot be disabled, ensuring RC control over EP application registers even without EP firmware. It also underpins MSI register access conventions in EP/RC interactions. Software must not attempt to remap BAR0 to other targets.
evidence: Section 2.7.2.2 BAR0 Exception; Application register map (3.1.1)
dependencies: BAR0 assigned and visible; inbound translation for BAR0 not used
Remarks: Use BAR0 for control-plane only; keep BAR0 window small and protected at system level.

feature_name: Peer-to-peer MSI to EP via BAR0 (implementation provision)
description: Although PCIe spec scopes MSIs to RC, the module allows generating an MSI-like write from one EP to another EP's BAR0 MSI_IRQ register. This is accomplished by targeting the peer's BAR0 space instead of RC memory. The receiver handles it like a local MSI vector event. System software must route address windows appropriately and ensure isolation.
dependencies: BAR0 visibility between peers; correct address translation on both EPs
Remarks: Use only in controlled topologies (e.g., behind a switch with P2P enabled); validate security/isolation externally.

feature_name: Link and transaction filtering controls
description: SYMTIMER_FLTMASK and FLT_MASK2 expose controls to drop or pass CFG/IO/MSG TLPs and adjust completion acceptance rules. They also set SKP intervals and can disable flow-control watchdogs. These masks help adapt the endpoint to system policies and workarounds. Misconfiguration can cause protocol violations or UR storms. Use them sparingly and document changes.
dependencies: Program mask bits only with deep protocol understanding
Remarks: Prefer standards-compliant defaults; audit changes during compliance testing.

feature_name: Power domain and module state transitions (PSC integration)
description: Turning the PCIe power domain off/on resets the module and clears link state. Software must stop traffic on both ends before gating power to avoid hangs, then perform full reinitialization and link training. PSC GOSTAT bits manage sequencing and status. There is no extra delay after power-on beyond normal initialization.
evidence: Section 2.13.3 Power Domain and Module State Transitions
dependencies: SoC PSC driver; link quiesce handshake with peer
Remarks: If hang occurs due to improper sequencing, apply device POR; design graceful drain paths before gating.

feature_name: Security capabilities
description: The user guide does not define explicit security, isolation, or cryptographic features within PCIESS. No access control lists, context-based permissions, or link encryption registers are specified. BAR windows and translation are functional, without security qualifiers. System-level firewalls and MPU/MPAX should enforce address isolation. Treat security policy as a platform concern outside PCIESS.
evidence: Absence of a Security section; Chapter 3 Registers (no security registers)
dependencies: System firewalls/MPU; secure boot and key storage external to PCIESS
Remarks: Harden by minimizing BAR exposure, using SoC firewalls, and validating inbound addresses in software.
